title: What a portfolio is — and what it is not
subtitle: A portfolio is a system with a purpose. A collection of investments without a defined objective is not a portfolio — it is a list.

body.content: The word portfolio is used loosely to mean any collection of investments. This is imprecise. A portfolio, properly understood, is a set of holdings constructed with a specific objective, a defined risk tolerance, and a coherent logic for how the parts interact. The difference between a portfolio and a list of investments is intention.

body.label: What defines a portfolio
body.content: A genuine portfolio has: a target return, an acceptable level of risk, a time horizon, a liquidity constraint, and a rebalancing rule. Without all five, you have a collection — and collections do not perform consistently because they were never designed to.

Most individual investors accumulate holdings reactively: they buy when they read something interesting, hold because selling feels like admitting a mistake, and add more when a tip comes from a trusted source. The result is a collection with overlapping exposures, no clear risk profile, and no coherent response to market events.

Portfolio construction is the discipline of designing holdings from the top down: starting with an objective, deriving an asset allocation, selecting instruments to implement that allocation, and maintaining it through systematic rules. It is architecture, not collection.
Before you evaluate any investment, ask what objective it serves in your portfolio. If you cannot answer that question, you are not constructing a portfolio. You are accumulating positions.
